title: Joan of Arc
summary: The peasant girl from Domrémy who claimed divine voices, persuaded the dauphin's court to give her a role in the Hundred Years' War, helped lift the siege of Orléans in 1429, and stood beside Charles VII at his coronation — then was captured, tried for heresy by a pro-English ecclesiastical court, and burned at Rouen at roughly nineteen. A posthumous retrial annulled the verdict in 1456; she was canonized in 1920. The trial transcripts survive in unusual completeness, making her one of the most documented people of the medieval world, yet the gap between the historical record and her layered mythic afterlife — Catholic martyr, French nationalist emblem, feminist icon, far-right mascot — has never closed.
hook: In May 1429, a seventeen-year-old farmer's daughter from a village most people had never heard of stood on the walls of a relieved Orléans and was recognized by soldiers who had just followed her into battle. She had arrived at the dauphin's court nine weeks earlier, unknown, carrying nothing but the claim that God had sent her. She left it with an army. No military rank, no noble title, no formal authority of any kind — and yet the siege broke.

beginner.theFigure:
In the spring of 1429, a seventeen-year-old girl walked into a room full of French nobles and told them God had sent her to save France. No one had ever heard of her. She had no title. She had no army. She had grown up tending animals in a small village near the eastern border of France. And yet within a few months, she had changed the direction of a war that had been grinding on for nearly a hundred years.

Her name was Joan. She came from a village called Domrémy. From around age thirteen, she later said, she had been hearing voices. She believed they were the voices of saints. They told her that the English had to be driven out of France, and that she was the one to help make it happen. This was not the kind of message people expected from a farmer's daughter. But Joan believed it completely, and that certainty carried her further than almost anyone thought possible.

She convinced a local captain to take her to the royal court. She convinced the court's examiners she was not a fraud. She convinced the dauphin Charles, the man who claimed to be France's rightful king, to give her armor and soldiers. Then she went to war.
beginner.worldInherited:
The France Joan was born into was in bad shape. England and France had been fighting each other off and on for nearly a century. This long struggle is called the Hundred Years' War. By the time Joan came along, the English controlled much of northern France, including the capital Paris. A powerful French faction called the Burgundians had allied with England against the French royal cause.

The man who should have been king, Charles VII, was called the dauphin. He had not yet been crowned because Reims, the city where French kings were always crowned, was held by his enemies. Some people questioned whether he was even the true heir. He was stuck in his own reduced territory, low on money and low on hope.

The English were now besieging the city of Orléans. If Orléans fell, the road south to Charles's remaining lands would be open. The whole situation looked desperate. Into this crisis Joan arrived, claiming she had been sent by heaven to fix things.
beginner.howExercisedPower:
Joan had no formal military rank. She could not vote on battle plans or overrule experienced commanders. What she had was something harder to measure: she made people believe the cause was not lost.

Soldiers noticed that she seemed afraid of nothing. She rode at the front, not safely at the rear. She was wounded by an arrow at Orléans and came back to fight the same day. Commanders who doubted her found that troops followed her more eagerly than they followed their official leaders.

She was also politically useful to Charles. Her claim that God supported his kingship helped answer the question of whether he was really the rightful heir. If heaven itself backed him, the argument against him seemed harder to make. When Charles was finally crowned at Reims in July 1429, Joan stood beside him with her banner. Her presence said, louder than any speech could, that this coronation had divine approval.
beginner.whatTheyChanged:
The relief of Orléans in May 1429 did not end the war. But it changed its feeling. The English and Burgundians had seemed unstoppable. Now they had been pushed back. French forces won several more battles in quick succession. Charles VII was crowned king. The idea that the English could simply take all of France started to look less certain.

Joan's own story ended badly. In May 1430 she was captured by Burgundian soldiers and sold to the English. They handed her to a church court led by a bishop who supported their cause. The trial that followed lasted months. She was accused of heresy and of dressing like a man. In May 1431, she was burned at the stake in Rouen. She was about nineteen years old.

Twenty-five years later, a new church court looked at the first trial again. It found that the trial had been rigged and that Joan had been wrongly condemned. Her reputation was restored. In 1920, the Catholic Church declared her a saint.
beginner.legendVsRecord:
Joan is one of the few people from the medieval world whose trial was written down in detail. Thousands of words she spoke during her questioning survive. So do the memories of people who knew her, collected years later for the retrial. This is unusually rich evidence for someone who lived six hundred years ago.

But Joan has also been claimed by so many different groups over the years that it can be hard to see the real person behind all the images. She has been painted as a perfect Catholic martyr. She has been turned into a symbol of French national pride. She has been celebrated as a brave young woman in a world that gave women no power. She has even been adopted by French nationalist movements whose politics she would have had no way to recognize.

None of these portraits is entirely wrong, but none is the whole picture either. The record shows a determined, clever young woman who held fast under tremendous pressure. It does not explain her voices. It does not say whether what she experienced was spiritual, something else, or simply beyond what the record can tell us. Some questions stay open.

general.theFigure:
When Jean de Metz, a minor nobleman at Vaucouleurs, asked Joan why she expected anyone to believe her, she reportedly answered that she must be at the king's side before mid-Lent, even if the journey wore her legs to the knees. She arrived at Charles VII's court at Chinon in late February 1429, as promised. What happened in the weeks that followed — a theological examination, a private audience with the dauphin, an assignment to the relief army — can only be explained if Joan persuaded the people around her with unusual force. The question worth sitting with is how a teenage commoner from a village no one had heard of managed this.

Joan came to Chinon at the lowest point of the Valois cause. Orléans was under siege, Paris was in English hands, and Charles's claim to the throne was genuinely contested. His father, Charles VI, had signed the Treaty of Troyes in 1420, legally disinheriting him in favor of the English king. The legal and moral basis of the French crown was fractured. Joan's claim that heaven endorsed Charles filled an urgent political need. No other argument available to the Valois court could do what hers did.
general.worldInherited:
The Hundred Years' War was not a single continuous conflict but a series of wars with long intervals, driven by competing claims to the French crown and bound up with the internal politics of both kingdoms. By the 1420s, English military success combined with the Burgundian alliance had placed large parts of France, including Paris and Normandy, under effective English control. The Treaty of Troyes formalized this by naming the young English king Henry VI as France's future ruler.

Beyond the military crisis lay a deeper problem of legitimate authority. Medieval political thought held that rule came ultimately from God. If the dauphin's legal claim was void under Troyes, only a demonstration of divine backing could override it. Joan's arrival offered exactly that: not a legal argument but a direct assertion that God had chosen Charles. The theologians who examined her at Poitiers spent three weeks testing her claim and found no grounds to reject it. That outcome mattered politically.

The Burgundian dimension shaped the longer arc. Duke Philip of Burgundy had allied with England after the murder of his father in 1419. Joan's military success and the coronation that followed helped create conditions for the Congress of Arras in 1435, where Burgundy eventually reconciled with the French crown. That reconciliation altered the war's trajectory more fundamentally than any single battle.
general.howExercisedPower:
Joan's formal position in the French army was unusual. She held an honorific kind of command and was given her own banner and household, but the actual planning of operations fell to experienced commanders like Jean de Dunois and the Duke of Alençon. What she brought was neither strategy nor tactical innovation. The historian Kelly DeVries, who has studied the Orléans campaign closely, concludes that tactical decisions during the siege's relief were made by French commanders, and that Joan's role was primarily motivational and symbolic.

But symbol and morale are real forces in war. Joan's contribution was to fuse them with an intensity professional soldiers could not replicate. She moved openly among the troops, insisted on religious discipline in camp, and carried a conviction that the fighting was righteous. When an arrow struck her during the assault on the Tourelles fortress, she had it removed and returned to the field before the day ended. Soldiers noticed. They talked about it. People who believe their cause is divinely supported fight with a different quality of effort.

Her political value to Charles was at least as great as her military role. The coronation at Reims in July 1429 transformed Charles from a disputed claimant into an anointed king. The ceremony was not merely ritual. It was the legal and religious act that settled the legitimacy question in a way no treaty could have done. Joan stood beside Charles in the cathedral holding her banner. Her presence was itself a statement: this coronation had heaven's approval.
general.whatTheyChanged:
Joan's direct military impact ran from the relief of Orléans in May 1429 through the coronation in July and into the Loire campaign that followed. The weeks after Orléans produced a run of Valois victories in rapid succession. The English and Burgundians had seemed to hold an overwhelming advantage, and that appearance fractured. The psychological shift was real on both sides.

After her capture in May 1430 and execution in May 1431, the war continued for another twenty-two years. The French expelled the English from nearly all of France by 1453. Joan did not win the war. What she did was help save the Valois monarchy at its lowest point and provide the coronation that stabilized Charles VII's claim. Charles, for his part, made no serious effort to ransom or rescue her. The omission was politically convenient for him as much as it was a personal failing.

Her longer significance lay in her afterlife. The retrial of 1456, ordered by Charles VII partly for his own political reasons, produced a formal record that cleared her name. The testimony gathered from dozens of witnesses over the decades after her death is an unusually rich archive. Her symbolic importance proved more durable than anything she achieved on the battlefield. She became a resource for French national identity. Successive generations projected their own political needs onto her — the Revolution, Napoleonic nationalism, Catholic conservatism, and twentieth-century movements across the spectrum all claimed her for their own purposes.
general.legendVsRecord:
The source situation for Joan is unusual for a medieval figure. The condemnation trial of 1431 was transcribed in Latin and French, and substantial portions survive. The nullification trial of 1456 gathered testimony from more than a hundred people who had known her. This is not the thin, fragmentary evidence historians normally work with for a fifteenth-century peasant.

What that evidence shows is a young woman of strong conviction and sharp intelligence, who held her own in cross-examination against trained clerical lawyers for months, who insisted on the reality of her voices under sustained pressure, and who recanted briefly at the end before withdrawing her recantation and going to the fire. The evidence does not show the military genius of later legend, nor the passive saint of early hagiography.

The gap between record and legend opened almost immediately after her death. Within a generation, writers were already constructing different Joans for different purposes. Marina Warner's study traces how each era refashioned her: the Renaissance produced a civic heroine, the Revolution a republican martyr, the nineteenth century a national saint coinciding with the rise of French nationalism, and the twentieth century a figure contested between Catholic tradition and secular republicanism. The far-right Action Française claimed her around the turn of the twentieth century; she is still invoked by the French far right today. None of these versions is simply invented — each finds something genuine in the record — but each imports the assumptions of its own moment into a life the evidence only partially makes legible.

advanced.theFigure:
The question of Joan's voices resists resolution, and that resistance is itself analytically significant. The trial record preserves her descriptions in some detail: she heard them first at around age thirteen, associated them with Saints Catherine and Margaret and with the Archangel Michael, and distinguished them from ordinary mental events by their accompaniment with light and their consistency of message. She maintained this account under sustained interrogation designed to expose it as either fabricated or diabolically inspired. The interrogators were not credulous men; Bishop Cauchon and Jean d'Estivet were trained canonists who understood exactly what it would take to demonstrate a false vision.

Modern attempts to explain the voices — temporal lobe epilepsy, ergotism from contaminated grain, bovine tuberculosis, schizophrenia — share a structural problem: they impose diagnostic categories developed five centuries after the fact onto an account filtered through trial transcript, translation, and judicial framing. What the record can support is narrower and more useful: Joan experienced something she interpreted as divine communication, she acted on that interpretation with complete consistency, and her experience was embedded in a culture that took seriously the possibility of genuine mystical contact. Régine Pernoud made the methodological point clearly: the relevant question is not what modern psychiatry might make of Joan's symptoms but what the fifteenth century made of them, and why the answers differed so sharply between those who found her credible and those who did not.
advanced.worldInherited:
The political crisis of the Valois monarchy in the 1420s had a specific ideological dimension that shaped how Joan's claims could function. The Treaty of Troyes did not merely transfer military advantage to England — it produced a genuine legal rupture in the theory of Valois legitimacy. Medieval political theology linked royal authority to divine election, expressed through coronation and dynastic succession. When both the legal basis of succession and the physical ceremony of coronation were denied Charles, the resulting vacuum was not simply political but theological.

This is why Joan's claim operated with force beyond what military morale alone can explain. She was not offering better tactics or more troops. She was offering a counter-theory of legitimacy — divine endorsement that could override treaty obligations — in exactly the terms the political culture required. The Poitiers examination, usually treated as an obstacle she had to pass, was in fact part of the legitimating mechanism: a formal church inquiry that found no grounds to condemn her was a kind of certification, however unofficial. The parallel with prophetic tradition was available to her contemporaries in ways it is easy to underestimate. Christine de Pizan, writing in 1429, compared Joan explicitly to biblical women who intervened in history at moments of crisis.
advanced.howExercisedPower:
The debate about Joan's actual military contribution has sharper edges than popular accounts allow. DeVries's close analysis of the Orléans campaign found that command decisions were made by Dunois and other experienced commanders, that Joan was sometimes excluded from planning councils, and that her push for direct assault on English fortifications was resisted by officers who preferred to wait. Her military role was real but not primarily technical — it operated at a different register than the operational choices of trained captains.

The more productive frame for her power may be what Max Weber called charismatic authority: legitimacy grounded not in tradition or legal office but in the perceived personal gift of the claimant. Weber's model fits the historical pattern closely. Joan's authority functioned precisely as long as outcomes confirmed her claim. When the Paris assault failed in September 1429 and the autumn campaign stalled, her influence contracted. Capture ended her charismatic function entirely, because no institutional structure existed to sustain it once she was a prisoner rather than a leader advancing on the field.

The charges at the 1431 trial illuminate what her opponents grasped about this. The central accusation was not military failure but the nature of her authority claims: that she had placed her private revelations above the Church's judgment, that she refused ecclesiastical supervision of her voices. The charge was not that she was wrong about the voices but that she had declined to let the institutional Church determine whether they were divine or diabolical. That refusal, maintained consistently until her brief recantation, was a claim to direct divine access that the Church could not absorb.
advanced.whatTheyChanged:
The historiographical debate about Joan's military significance has a second, deeper argument embedded in it: whether the turn in the Hundred Years' War was caused by her actions or was already underway for structural reasons she accelerated but did not initiate. Revisionist military historians have noted that French military capacity had been improving through the 1420s and that the English overextension was producing political problems at home regardless of events in France. On this reading, Joan arrived at a moment when the balance was already shifting and her contribution was to accelerate a trajectory rather than reverse one.

The problem with the purely structuralist account is that it cannot explain why Orléans was relieved when it was relieved, why the coronation happened when it did, or why the morale shift among French and Burgundian populations occurred so rapidly after her military success. Structure constrains events; it does not produce them on a specific calendar. The more defensible position is that Joan operated within enabling conditions she did not create — English political overstretch, French military recovery, Burgundian ambivalence — and that her intervention at a particular moment genuinely altered the sequence rather than merely rode it. The coronation at Reims is the clearest case: Charles's legitimacy was materially different after July 1429 than before it, and that difference had political consequences that outlasted the war.
advanced.legendVsRecord:
The myth-layering problem for Joan is more complex than the standard account of posthumous distortion allows, because the mythologizing began during her lifetime and she participated in it. Her self-presentation drew on available cultural scripts — the virgin warrior, the prophetic peasant girl, the instrument of divine will — that her contemporaries would have recognized from scripture, hagiography, and popular tradition. She was not simply reporting unmediated experience; she was framing her experience in forms that made it legible and politically functional.

This creates a layered interpretive problem that pure source criticism cannot dissolve. The trial transcripts, treated by Pernoud and others as access to the authentic Joan, are themselves products of a judicial process designed to elicit specific kinds of admission. Joan's answers were constrained not just by the need to defend herself but by the theological vocabulary available for discussing spiritual experience. When she says she recognized the voices as those of Saint Catherine and Margaret, she may be reporting genuine experience, or she may be fitting that experience into the only categories her culture provided, or both simultaneously in a way the distinction cannot capture.

The modern political appropriations of Joan — from Action Française in the late 1890s to the contemporary Front National and its successors — raise a distinct but related problem. These appropriations do not merely distort a recoverable original; they compete to claim interpretive authority over a figure whose power has always been partly constituted by the projection of values onto her. Marina Warner's argument that 'each century has found a different use for her' is correct as description but conceals a harder question: whether there is a residue of historical Joan that these projections cannot exhaust, or whether the only Joan available is always the Joan of a particular interpretive moment. The trial transcripts suggest the former. The ease with which radically incompatible movements have each found their Joan suggests the latter.

foundation.theStory: Joan of Arc presents two distinct historical problems that sit inside each other. The first is the problem of an individual's impact on a specific military and political crisis: what exactly did a teenage commoner contribute to the relief of Orléans, the coronation at Reims, and the ultimate Valois victory in the Hundred Years' War? The second is the problem of evidence and myth: how should historians work with a figure whose trial records are unusually rich but whose afterlife is so heavily mythologized that separating historical reality from cultural projection requires constant vigilance? The two problems are connected. The reasons Joan's image became so contested are partly rooted in the ambiguities the historical record itself leaves open.
foundation.theDebate: Historians disagree on three connected questions. First, the extent of Joan's independent military judgment versus her primarily symbolic and motivational role — with Kelly DeVries and military historians emphasizing the tactical competence of commanders like Dunois, while others argue Joan made real operational contributions. Second, the nature of her voices: whether her spiritual experiences should be bracketed as epistemically inaccessible, explained psychologically, or taken seriously as genuine mystical experience within her cultural framework. Third, what the Hundred Years' War's eventual outcome owes to Joan specifically versus longer-term structural factors. A fourth debate, less historiographical than political, concerns the legitimate versus illegitimate uses of her image — who may invoke her as a symbol and in what context.
foundation.whyItMatters: Joan of Arc matters for at least four distinct fields of inquiry. She is a case study in charismatic authority — how someone with no institutional standing acquires and exercises real power. She is a window into the political theology of medieval monarchy and what divine legitimation meant in practice. She is one of the most documented medieval women, making her central to gender history's reconstruction of female agency. And she is an unusually rich example of how historical figures are mythologized — the successive layers of Joan (martyr, nationalist saint, feminist icon, political football) illuminate how modern nations manufacture usable pasts.
questions: Joan's authority over soldiers and courtiers rested on her claim to divine mission. What does it mean for a society to take such claims seriously — and what conditions made that kind of authority possible in the fifteenth century that might not exist in the same form today?

The 1431 trial convicted Joan; the 1456 retrial annulled the verdict. Both were conducted by the institutional Church. What does it tell us about ecclesiastical authority that the same institution could produce both outcomes? What changed between 1431 and 1456, and why does that matter for how we evaluate both trials?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The scholarly conversation divides roughly into three traditions. The French Catholic tradition, represented most accessibly by Régine Pernoud, treats the trial record as substantially reliable access to Joan's actual character and argues for reading her on her own terms — as someone who genuinely experienced divine communication, whatever explanation one assigns to it, and whose condemnation was a judicial murder. The British and American military-historical tradition, represented by Kelly DeVries and others, focuses on what Joan actually did operationally and tends to deflate both the miracle and the military genius, finding a young woman whose contribution was real but more limited than legend suggests. Marina Warner's Alone of All Her Sex and Joan of Arc: The Image of Female Heroism represent a third tradition: cultural history focused on the afterlife of her image, which treats the successive mythologizations as themselves historically significant rather than distortions to be stripped away. These three traditions ask different questions and largely talk past each other.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The trial transcripts — both the 1431 condemnation and the 1456 nullification proceedings — are unusually detailed for a medieval figure. But both are products of legal processes with strong incentives shaping the testimony. The 1431 trial was designed to convict; the 1456 retrial was designed to rehabilitate. Neither is neutral documentation. What the transcripts establish: the general course of Joan's questioning, her characteristic responses, the theological charges leveled against her, and the voices as she described them. What they cannot establish: whether her descriptions accurately report her experience, whether the voices had a neurological or spiritual basis, and whether the accounts of witnesses at the 1456 proceedings reflect memory or reconstruction. The military record is thinner — contemporary chronicles exist but their reliability varies, and the tactical details of the Orléans campaign remain disputed.
interpretation.takingAPosition:
A defensible scholarly position on Joan must hold together several things that pull in different directions. On her military role: the most honest account acknowledges that the relief of Orléans involved experienced commanders and that Joan's direct tactical contribution is difficult to isolate from her symbolic and motivational function — but that symbol and morale are real military forces, not secondary ones, and that her role cannot be dismissed as merely ceremonial. A strong argument would specify what changed between the failed earlier relief attempts and the May 1429 success, and assess Joan's causal contribution to that change against the background of other changing variables.

On her voices: the responsible position is to hold the epistemological question open rather than force resolution. Pernoud's insistence on reading Joan within her own cultural framework is methodologically sound; so is the refusal to diagnose across five centuries with categories she could not have recognized. A strong argument acknowledges that what Joan experienced was real to her, that her culture provided the only available language for it, and that what the language reveals and conceals about the underlying experience cannot be recovered.

On her significance: the key move is to distinguish between what Joan directly caused and what she enabled or accelerated. The coronation at Reims is the clearest case of direct causation — it would not have happened when it did without her insistence and her military success. The eventual Valois victory in 1453 cannot be attributed to her; the structural conditions for it were developing independently. The honest claim is that she altered the sequence of events at a critical moment in ways that the counterfactual cannot fully reverse.
questions: The 1431 trial convicted Joan on charges including heresy and refusing Church authority over her visions; the 1456 retrial annulled the verdict using largely the same canonical framework. Both outcomes were politically motivated. What does this reveal about the relationship between legal process, institutional authority, and political power in the medieval Church — and how should historians evaluate verdicts produced under these conditions?

Régine Pernoud argues that Joan should be read within her own cultural framework, treating her voices as a genuine religious experience rather than a symptom to be diagnosed. Marina Warner focuses on the mythologizations that followed rather than the historical person. Kelly DeVries examines military operations. Each approach produces a different Joan. Which questions can each method answer, and which questions does each method structurally prevent itself from asking?

research.topic: The trial record and its scope
research.content: The condemnation trial of 1431 was transcribed in both Latin and French; substantial portions survive in multiple manuscript copies, including Joan's direct answers to her interrogators over months of questioning. The nullification trial of 1456 gathered testimony from more than one hundred witnesses who had known Joan at various stages of her life. Together these documents constitute an unusually complete evidentiary record for a medieval commoner. Scholars including Régine Pernoud and W.P. Barrett produced critical editions and translations in the twentieth century. The authenticity of the core transcripts is not seriously disputed; their interpretation is.

research.topic: Joan's role in the military operations at Orléans
research.content: Military historians including Kelly DeVries have reexamined the operational record of the Orléans campaign in detail, drawing on contemporary siege accounts and chronicles alongside the trial testimony. The emerging scholarly picture emphasizes the tactical competence of Dunois and other experienced commanders and argues that Joan's contribution was primarily motivational and symbolic rather than operationally decisive. This interpretation remains contested by historians who read the sources as assigning Joan direct command decisions, particularly the assault on the Tourelles fortress. Ongoing work in archival sources not fully integrated into standard accounts continues to refine the picture.

What evidence would resolve this: contemporary medical or neurological records documenting Joan's condition, or private correspondence in which she described her experiences outside the constrained framework of judicial interrogation. Why such evidence is unavailable: no private records survive; the only substantive descriptions are filtered through trial transcripts produced under adversarial legal conditions in a theological vocabulary that structured what could be said. What can be responsibly inferred: Joan experienced something she interpreted as divine communication, maintained that interpretation under sustained pressure designed to break it, and the experience was consistent and directive rather than random. What must remain unknowable: whether the voices had a neurological basis identifiable to modern medicine, whether they represented genuine mystical experience in any theologically meaningful sense, and what relationship her conscious descriptions bear to the underlying experience. Medical retrospective diagnosis — various scholars have proposed epilepsy, bovine tuberculosis, and other conditions — cannot be established from the available evidence and imposes modern categories onto medieval self-description.

comparativeNarrative.name: Witch, Heretic, and Political Threat
comparativeNarrative.content: From the English and Burgundian perspective, Joan was dangerous precisely because her claims could not be easily dismissed. The response was not to argue that she was wrong about the military situation but to delegitimize her authority by characterizing it as diabolical rather than divine. Bishop Cauchon's trial was not a dispassionate inquiry but a political operation — designed to retrospectively justify the English cause and to discredit Charles VII's coronation by showing that his legitimacy rested on a convicted heretic. The charges of cross-dressing and refusing Church authority were canonically precise tools for achieving a predetermined verdict. That the trial's conclusion was driven by political necessity rather than theological finding is precisely what the 1456 retrial established.

comparativeNarrative.name: Martyr, Saint, and National Protector
comparativeNarrative.content: The French Catholic tradition read Joan as a providential figure whose life and death followed the pattern of martyrdom: divine calling, mission accomplished despite opposition, betrayal, unjust condemnation, and death that confirmed rather than refuted the mission's validity. The canonization campaign that culminated in 1920 built on this framework, situating Joan in the lineage of saints who died for truth. This reading genuinely captures something in the record: Joan maintained her convictions to the end, and the retrial endorsed the view that the condemnation was fraudulent. What it elides is the political instrumentalization of her memory by the French state and nationalist movements, and the gap between the actual medieval woman and the timeless saint the tradition constructed.

comparativeNarrative.name: A Symbol Claimed Across the Political Spectrum
comparativeNarrative.content: From the French Revolution onward, Joan has been a contested national symbol rather than a confessional one. Republicans invoked her against the Church; Catholics invoked her against republicans. The Action Française in the early twentieth century made her a symbol of anti-republican nationalism; the Resistance invoked her against German occupation. The contemporary far right treats her as a symbol of ethnic French identity and resistance to immigration. The French left has at times reclaimed her as a figure of popular resistance against elite power. This spectrum of appropriation reveals that Joan does not have a natural political valence — her usefulness as a symbol lies precisely in the ambiguities the historical record leaves open, which each tradition fills with its own content.

rabbitHole.label: Medieval European Society
rabbitHole.reason: The political theology of medieval kingship — the idea that royal authority derived from divine election, expressed through coronation and confirmed by dynastic succession — was the framework within which Joan's claims made sense and exerted force. Understanding what legitimacy meant in 1429 is prerequisite to understanding why her mission worked.

rabbitHole.label: Christianity
rabbitHole.reason: Joan's authority rested entirely on Christian theological categories: divine visions, saintly intercession, the institutional Church's role in adjudicating religious experience. The trial that killed her and the retrial that vindicated her were both conducted within ecclesiastical jurisdiction. The Christian framework is not context for Joan's story — it is its substance.

rabbitHole.label: The Protestant Reformation
rabbitHole.reason: Joan's trial turned on whether the individual could claim direct divine authority against the institutional Church's judgment — a question the Reformation would place at the center of European politics a century later. Her insistence that her private revelation superseded ecclesiastical supervision was, in theological structure, a precursor to arguments Protestants would later make on much broader grounds.

rabbitHole.label: Nationalism
rabbitHole.reason: Joan is one of the earliest figures retrospectively recruited into nationalist symbolism, and her repeated reinvention as a French national icon across three centuries illustrates how nationalist movements construct usable pasts. The gap between the medieval woman who fought for the Valois dynasty and the nineteenth-century national heroine fighting for France is a case study in how nationalism rewrites history.

rabbitHole.label: Gender
rabbitHole.reason: Joan exercised military and political authority in a world that formally denied women both. The charge of cross-dressing at her trial was not incidental but central — it named what made her threatening to the social order her opponents wanted to defend. Her afterlife as a feminist symbol engages genuinely with this dimension, even if it imports modern frameworks that require scrutiny.

rabbitHole.label: Legitimacy
rabbitHole.reason: The crisis Joan entered was primarily a crisis of legitimacy: who had the right to rule France, on what grounds, and how that right could be established or contested. Her specific contribution was to supply a form of legitimacy — divine endorsement — that the existing legal and dynastic frameworks could not produce. The coronation at Reims was the culmination of that contribution.

rabbitHole.label: Martin Luther
rabbitHole.reason: Both Joan and Luther defied the institutional Church by claiming direct access to divine authority, and both had that claim adjudicated in formal ecclesiastical trials. The structural parallel illuminates how the medieval Church understood and responded to challenges from below — and how much depends on political context in determining which challenges become heresies and which become reformations.

rabbitHole.label: Marie Curie
rabbitHole.reason: Joan and Curie both achieved in fields formally closed to women and became symbols of female capability across different eras. The contrast between a medieval woman whose authority required divine framing to be legible and a modern scientist whose authority was grounded in secular expertise illuminates how the available frameworks for female public power changed between the fifteenth and twentieth centuries.

reference.author: Pernoud, Régine and Clin, Marie-Véronique
reference.title: Joan of Arc: Her Story
reference.annotation: The essential scholarly biography by France's foremost Joan of Arc historian — Pernoud's rigorous engagement with the trial transcripts and her insistence on reading Joan within her own cultural framework set the standard for subsequent scholarship. The English translation is the starting point for serious engagement with the primary evidence.

reference.author: Warner, Marina
reference.title: Joan of Arc: The Image of Female Heroism
reference.annotation: The indispensable account of Joan's cultural afterlife — Warner traces how each era has refashioned Joan's image to fit its own needs, revealing what the successive mythologizations tell us about gender, nationalism, and the politics of historical memory. Essential counterweight to source-focused accounts.

reference.author: DeVries, Kelly
reference.title: Joan of Arc: A Military Leader
reference.annotation: The most rigorous military-historical assessment of Joan's operational role — DeVries examines the siege of Orléans and subsequent campaigns in tactical detail, distinguishing Joan's actual military contributions from the roles of experienced commanders. Essential corrective to both hagiographic and dismissive accounts of her military significance.

reference.author: Taylor, Larissa Juliet
reference.title: The Virgin Warrior: The Life and Death of Joan of Arc
reference.annotation: A recent scholarly biography that synthesizes the military history, theological context, and cultural reception in a single readable account — useful for readers who want the full picture without multiple monographs, and reliable on the trial evidence and its limits.

reference.author: Barrett, W.P. (translator)
reference.title: The Trial of Jeanne d'Arc
reference.annotation: An English translation of the condemnation trial transcript — the essential primary source, Joan's own words as recorded under interrogation. Read critically (the record was produced under judicial pressure) but unavoidable for any serious engagement with what Joan actually said and how she argued her case.

commerce.title: Joan of Arc: A Life Transfigured
commerce.author: Kathryn Harrison
commerce.note: Start here if you have no prior background — Harrison's biography is beautifully written and takes Joan seriously as a person without requiring the reader to navigate scholarly debates; the right first book for a curious general reader.

commerce.title: Joan of Arc: Her Story
commerce.author: Régine Pernoud and Marie-Véronique Clin
commerce.note: The essential next step — Pernoud's rigorous but readable scholarship grounds Joan in the trial transcripts and the political crisis she entered; for readers who want to understand what the historical record actually shows rather than the legend.

commerce.title: Joan of Arc: The Image of Female Heroism
commerce.author: Marina Warner
commerce.note: Read this alongside Pernoud if you want to understand not just who Joan was but how she became the symbol she is — Warner's cultural history of Joan's afterlife is essential for anyone who has encountered the nationalist and religious mythology and wants to know what it reveals about the cultures that built it.

commerce.title: Joan of Arc: A Military Leader
commerce.author: Kelly DeVries
commerce.note: The best scholarly account of Joan's actual military role — for readers with some background in medieval history who want to know what she did operationally and how that relates to experienced commanders like Dunois; essential for separating military reality from legend.

commerce.title: The Virgin Warrior: The Life and Death of Joan of Arc
commerce.author: Larissa Juliet Taylor
commerce.note: The best single scholarly synthesis for a reader who wants military history, theological context, and cultural reception in one volume — Taylor integrates the scholarship accessibly without sacrificing rigor; for readers ready to move beyond the introductory accounts.

popularCulture.title: The Passion of Joan of Arc — Carl Theodor Dreyer (1928)
popularCulture.description: Dreyer's silent film, built almost entirely from close-ups of Maria Falconetti's face during the trial, captures something the historical record also emphasizes: Joan's psychological endurance under sustained interrogation. What it distorts: the film compresses the lengthy trial into a single day and gives it an emotional intensity that the actual months-long proceeding, with its repetitive procedural questioning, did not have. One of the great films, but not a documentary.

popularCulture.title: Joan of Arc — Luc Besson (1999)
popularCulture.description: Besson's visually spectacular version starring Milla Jovovich tries to hold open the question of Joan's sanity while delivering an action film. What it gets right: the chaos and violence of medieval siege warfare. What it distorts: a late scene suggesting Joan may have been deluded imposes modern psychological skepticism that neither the historical record nor the period supports — it reads as a contemporary filmmaker's discomfort with religious conviction, not an insight into Joan.

popularCulture.title: Saint Joan — George Bernard Shaw (1923)
popularCulture.description: Shaw's play established the dominant twentieth-century secular reading of Joan: a proto-Protestant individualist whose insistence on personal conscience over institutional authority made her a precursor of modernity. The framing captures something real about how her trial worked. What it imposes: Shaw's Joan is far more intellectually self-aware of her historical role than any medieval teenager could have been; the play is Shaw's argument about modern individualism, dressed in fifteenth-century costume.

popularCulture.title: Personal Recollections of Joan of Arc — Mark Twain (1896)
popularCulture.description: Twain considered this his finest work. Written as the memoir of Joan's childhood companion, it presents her as a figure of pure moral heroism — unusually reverent for Twain. It captures the injustice of her trial with genuine anger. What it distorts: the portrait is idealized to hagiography, stripping away theological complexity and political instrumentalization — the very dimensions that make the historical Joan more interesting than the saint.

popularCulture.title: Joan of Arc as far-right political symbol
popularCulture.description: Since the Action Française in the 1890s, the French far right has claimed Joan as a symbol of ethnic French identity and resistance to outside influence — a tradition the Front National and its successors continue. This distorts the medieval woman, who fought for the Valois dynasty rather than an ethnic nation, while exploiting genuine ambiguities in her image. The conversion of a medieval martyr into ethnonationalist symbolism is itself a significant historical phenomenon.

popularCulture.title: The image of Joan of Arc in French national memory
popularCulture.description: Joan's image has been invoked by the Revolution, Napoleon, Vichy, the Resistance, and the contemporary left and right — often simultaneously by opposing sides. This breadth of appropriation demonstrates how national symbols work and why the historical record's genuine opacity is useful rather than inconvenient for ideological purposes. Each movement finds something real in the record; each also imports its own assumptions. The result maps the internal contradictions of French national identity with unusual completeness.
